videos.title: Best Things To Do in Bali 2026 4K
videos.description:
Are you looking for the best things to do in Bali? We just created a list of 35 must do activities for you to do while visiting Bali Indonesia! In this Bali travel guide we show you around Kuta, Uluwatu, Sanur, Canggu, Ubud, Seminyak and many other top tourist destinations in Bali.  Some of those Bali attractions include Tanah Lot, The Rock Bar, Amed Beach, Lombok, Gili Islands, Beach Clubs, Surf Schools, Temples, Cultural villages, Garuda Wisnu Temple and much more!  If you enjoy this Bali travel video you can also watch our other travel vlogs from across Indonesia.

It took 10 days to make this Bali travel video. I would say you can explore most of Bali in the southern part of the island around Kuta Legian and Uluwatu in 3 to 5 days but if you want to see Sanur, Seminyak and Canggu you will need more time. Mostly because Bali traffic can be quite dense. You can easily do these attractions on your own and don't need a guide but I would recommend a guide for first time visitors. Make sure you find a registered tour guide and company on the island. You can find the best Bali in Kuta, Nusa Dua, Seminyak and Uluwatu. The best food in Bali seems to be Nasi Goreng as it is the most popular with most locals.

videos.transcript: Landing in Bali for the first time can feel a bit chaotic if you don’t know what to expect. Some travelers get stuck in long immigration lines, others forget to fill out a form or end up paying more than they should for a taxi. And depending on when you land and how prepared you are, getting out of the airport can take anywhere from twenty minutes to over two hours. So in this video, I’m going to walk you through exactly how to get through Bali’s international airport the smart way. I’ll explain what you need to do before your flight, how the arrival process works once you land, and what to watch out for so you don’t waste time or money. We’ll go step by step through immigration, baggage claim, customs, internet, currency exchange, and transport to your hotel. By the end of this video, you’ll know exactly what to expect and how to move through each part smoothly, even if it’s your first time on the island. So let’s get started. Before you even board your flight, there are a few things you need to take care of. And doing these in advance can make a huge difference when you land. First, check your passport. It needs to be valid for at least six months from the day you arrive in Indonesia. And it can’t have any damage. That means no water stains, no torn pages, not even a small rip. Bali is known for being unusually strict about this. Most travelers from countries like the United States, the United Kingdom, Australia, and most of Europe will need a visa to enter Indonesia. The good news is, there are two easy ways to get it. You can apply online before your trip, or just get it when you land. If you do it in advance, it’s called the electronic Visa on Arrival. It costs the same as getting it at the airport, around 35 dollars. The difference is that doing it online lets you skip the visa line entirely, which can be a huge time-saver. During low season, that line might take 30 minutes. But in high season, especially around July, August, or the holidays, the wait can stretch to two hours or more. So applying online is definitely the better option if you want to move through immigration faster. When filling out the electronic Visa on Arrival form, make sure to choose the B1 visa type, which is for tourism. You’ll need to upload a photo, fill in your passport details, your flight info, and where you’ll be staying. Once everything’s complete and approved, your electronic Visa on Arrival gets sent to your email. Just keep a copy on your phone or printed out, in case they ask for it when you arrive. If you prefer to get your visa when you arrive, no problem. Just head to the Visa on Arrival counter before immigration, pay the same fee, and hold onto the receipt because you’ll need to show it at passport control. Another thing you’ll need to fill out is the electronic customs declaration. This form is required for every traveler entering Indonesia, and it’s pretty straightforward. You can either fill it out individually or have one person complete it for the whole family. Just keep in mind that the form only opens two days before your flight. The system won’t let you submit it earlier, so there’s no point trying to fill it out a week in advance. Once it’s done, you’ll get a QR code. Save it to your phone or just take a screenshot. You don’t need to print anything. When you arrive, an officer will scan that code after you pick up your bags. If you’re not bringing anything that needs to be declared, the whole process usually takes less than a minute. If you don’t fill it out ahead of time, there are computers available at the airport to do it on the spot. There’s also a new fee called the Bali tourist tax. It’s officially known as the International Tourism Levy, and it applies to all foreign visitors. The cost is 150,000 rupiah per person, and you can pay it online before your trip through the Love Bali website or the official app. Just like the other steps we’ve talked about, this is something you can also do at the airport after you land. Right after payment, you’ll get a confirmation with a QR code. Most travelers won’t be asked for it when they arrive at the airport, and that’s totally normal. But in Bali, it’s common for staff at major tourist attractions to check if you’ve paid. The last document you’ll need to know about before flying is the health declaration form. This was introduced because of the recent Mpox outbreak, and it’s now required for all international arrivals in Indonesia. You’ll need to fill it out within a couple of days before your flight. The form asks for your flight details, the countries you’ve visited in the last 21 days, and whether you’ve had any symptoms related to Mpox. As soon as you send the form, you’ll receive another QR code. Just save it to your phone like the others. At the airport, most travelers go straight through without any issues, but if you report symptoms or recent exposure, you might be asked to go through additional screening, which could include a PCR test. So it’s another simple form that’s quick to fill out, and once it’s done, you’re all set for arrival. Before we get into the arrival process and how immigration works, let’s talk about SafetyWing. This is a travel insurance made for digital nomads, backpackers, or anyone taking an extended trip. It’s one of the best options out there if you’re staying abroad for a while. It covers medical emergencies, trip interruptions, lost luggage, motorbike accidents which are pretty common in Bali, and even flight delays. You can also upgrade your plan to include adventure sports or add extra protection for your gear, like cameras and laptops. It works in over 180 countries, and you can sign up even if you’re already traveling. Plus, claims are quick and straightforward with no complicated paperwork. If you want to travel with peace of mind, scan the QR code on the screen or click the link in the description to get covered with SafetyWing today. As soon as you arrive in Bali, the first thing you’ll go through is immigration. If you already applied for the electronic Visa on Arrival, you can skip the Visa on Arrival counter and go straight to the immigration area. For travelers who didn’t do it online, you’ll need to stop at the Visa on Arrival counter first, pay the visa fee, and then move on. If you’re using a biometric passport, you’ll be able to use the electronic gates. These are usually faster, but they only work if your passport meets the requirements and the system is working properly. Just follow the signs, place your passport on the scanner, and look at the camera. If everything checks out, the gates will open automatically. If there’s any issue, an officer will direct you to the manual immigration line. After immigration, you’ll head to baggage claim. If you’re traveling with carry-on only, you can skip this part completely. But if you checked a bag, just know that baggage claim in Bali can be slow. It’s not uncommon to wait 20 or even 30 minutes for your luggage to show up. With your bags in hand, the next stop is customs. This is where you’ll need that QR code from the electronic customs declaration you filled out earlier. Just pull it up on your phone, and an officer will scan it. This part usually takes less than a minute. Once you’re through immigration and customs, you’ll walk straight into the arrivals hall. And this is where you can take care of a few important things before heading to your hotel. First up, cash. If you didn’t bring any Indonesian rupiah with you, this is a good moment to withdraw some. The ATMs here are run by major Indonesian banks and are generally safe to use. In most cases, there aren’t any extra fees from the ATM itself, but it still depends on your bank back home. Right next to the ATMs, there are currency exchange counters as well. The rates aren’t the best you’ll find in Bali, but you won’t get scammed either, so it’s a safe option if you need some cash right away. Next is internet. If you didn’t get an eSIM before arriving, you’ll find several kiosks selling local SIM cards right there in the arrivals hall. The three most common providers are Telkomsel, XL, and Smartfren. Telkomsel usually has the strongest coverage across the island, especially in more remote areas. XL and Smartfren are also solid choices, often with better deals on data packages. All the kiosks are side by side, so it’s easy to compare prices before deciding which one to go with. You’ll need your passport to register the SIM, and the staff will usually help you get everything set up on your phone right there. After you’ve sorted your cash and internet, it’s time to head to your hotel. If your hotel arranged a private transfer, your driver will be waiting outside with a sign. But if not, you’ve got two main options: grab a local taxi or order a ride through an app. Local taxis are the least recommended option here. You’ll see a line of drivers holding signs and calling out for rides, but most of them don’t use a meter and will throw out inflated prices. The better move is to order a ride through Grab. It’s one of the two big ride apps in Indonesia, similar to Uber, and it’s super easy to use. Grab even has its own official lounge just outside the terminal, where drivers are allowed to pick up passengers. The lounge is air-conditioned, has seating, and is a nice place to wait while your car arrives. Right outside the lounge, there’s usually a stand where you can grab a discount voucher for your first ride, which makes it even cheaper. That’s everything you need to know to get through the airport without stress. If you found this video helpful, consider subscribing to the channel. It helps the channel grow and keeps me motivated to keep bringing you more quality travel guides. And if you’re thinking about getting travel insurance, the link for SafetyWing is in the description. Thanks for watching, and I’ll see you in the next one.

videos.transcript: Welcome to our video on the top 10 must-see destinations on the picturesque island of Bali. This Indonesian island, located at the westernmost end of the Lesser Sunda Islands, is home to various cultural hotspots, natural wonders, and popular tourist destinations. With Denpasar as its provincial capital and situated between Java and Lombok, Bali attracts millions of visitors annually. In this video, we'll take you on a journey to discover some of the best places to visit on the island. So, get comfortable and start planning your next adventure to Bali. Number 1 Ubud: Known as the cultural center of Bali, Ubud offers stunning rice terraces, traditional Balinese temples, and delicious local cuisine. The town is located in the island's central hills and is a great place to escape the hustle and bustle of the more touristy areas. Visitors can explore the many art galleries and museums, such as the Neka Art Museum, the Agung Rai Museum of Art, and the Museum Puri Lukisan, to gain insight into the island's rich artistic heritage. The Ubud Royal Palace and the Ubud Market are also must-see attractions where you can immerse yourself in the local culture and traditions. Take a walk through the market and find various traditional crafts and souvenirs. Number 2 Uluwatu:Uluwatu is one of the most popular surf destinations in all of Bali due to the making of Morning of the Earth, a 1971 classic surf film. If you are not a fan of surfing you can take a leisurely walk along the cliffs and admire the breathtaking ocean views. Its cliffside temple offers stunning views and traditional Balinese dances during sunset. The temple, situated at the southernmost point of the island, is revered as one of Bali's six most significant temples. The temple is believed to protect Bali against evil and is also home to a sizable population of monkeys inhabiting nearby forests. It's a unique experience to see them interacting with visitors, but keep your belongings safe, as they can be pretty cheeky. Number 3 Tanah Lot: Another coastal temple, Tanah Lot, is a popular spot for sunset and is said to be one of the most important temples in Bali. The temple is on a large rock formation surrounded by the ocean, making it a picturesque location. Visitors can also enjoy the beautiful surroundings by walking along the beach or watching traditional Balinese dances. The temple is also home to a beautiful and tranquil garden where visitors can explore the many statues and shrines. It's also said to be one of the most photographed temples in Bali. If you like golf visit the Nirwana Bali Golf Club designed by legendary pro golfer Greg Norman. Number 4 Kuta - A popular sun and beach tourist spot, Kuta offers excellent shopping, dining, and nightlife. For those looking for a more laid-back experience, Kuta also offers many yoga and meditation centers. Additionally, the beach is home to the famous Kuta beach break, one of Bali's most consistent surf breaks. Number 5 Seminyak: A step up from Kuta in terms of luxury, Seminyak offers high-end boutiques and resorts. The beach is a prime location for surfers of all abilities. Visitors can also take a leisurely walk along the beach promenade, checking out the various shops, bars, and restaurants. Kuta is also renowned for its vibrant nightlife scene, featuring many bars and clubs. Visitors can also explore the many art galleries, such as the W Hotel's Woobar and the Biasa Art Space, which feature works by some of Bali's most talented contemporary artists. Number 6 Nusa Dua - This area is known for its beautiful beaches and luxury resorts, making it the perfect spot for a relaxing vacation. Visitors can enjoy many water activities like snorkeling, diving, and sailing. The area is also home to the Bali Collection, an extensive shopping and dining complex that offers a variety of international and local brands. Nusa Dua is also home to the Bali National Golf Club, a must-visit for golf enthusiasts, which offers a challenging 18-hole course. Number 7 Jimbaran: This coastal town is famous for its seafood BBQs on the beach. Visitors can enjoy a delicious fresh seafood meal while watching the sunset over the ocean. The city is also known for its traditional fishing village atmosphere, where visitors can watch the local fishermen bring in their catch of the day. The city is also home to the beautiful Balangan Beach, a secluded spot that offers a peaceful retreat from the more touristy areas. Number 8 Canggu: A popular spot for surfers, Canggu also offers excellent cafes and restaurants. The area is also known for its laid-back vibe and bohemian atmosphere. Visitors can enjoy some of the best surf spots in Bali, such as Echo Beach and Batu Bolong Beach, take a yoga class or explore the many art galleries. Visitors can also explore the many rice fields and traditional villages scattered throughout the region. Number 9 Gili Islands: The Gili Islands, located just off the northwest coast of Lombok, offer crystal clear waters and excellent snorkeling and diving. Visitors can discover the diverse marine life and coral reefs while relishing in the island's relaxed atmosphere. The Gili Islands boast picturesque beaches like Gili Trawangan, Gili Air, and Gili Meno. Tourists can also visit a variety of small shops and restaurants that are dispersed throughout the islands. The Gili Islands are also a great place to learn how to scuba dive, as the waters are home to a diverse array of marine life and coral reefs. Number 10 Sanur: Known for its laid-back atmosphere, Sanur offers beautiful beaches and traditional markets. Visitors can walk along the beach promenade and explore the many shops, bars, and restaurants. The area is also home to many conventional Balinese temples and is a great place to learn about the island's culture. Sanur is also home to the Le Mayeur Museum, which showcases the works of Belgian painter Adrien-Jean Le Mayeur, who lived in Bali for many years. Visitors can also take a bike tour around the village and explore the many traditional markets and crafts centers. Bali is a paradise island that offers something for everyone. From stunning rice terraces, traditional Balinese temples, and delicious local cuisine to beautiful beaches, luxury resorts, and exciting nightlife. Remember to visit the temples, enjoy the delicious local cuisine and engage with the friendly local people. It's a once-in-a-lifetime experience, but do not forget that drug possession and use is harshly prohibited by Indonesian laws . We hope this video has given you a good starting point for planning your own Bali adventure and inspired you to visit this beautiful island. Thanks for watching, and remember to subscribe for more travel content. Bali's international airport in Dempasar is modern, efficient, and for most nationalities. You get a visa-free stay for up to 30 days. As you exit the airport, you can grab a local SIM card for $5 and book a ride using the Grab app, Bali's version of Uber. Start your journey in Uluatu in southern Bali, home to some of the island's best beaches and epic cliffside views. [Music] Renting a scooter costs around $5 per day and it gives you full freedom. Visit Padang Padang Beach made famous by the movie Eat Pray Love. [Music] For the perfect sunset, head to Ulu Watu Temple perched on the edge of a dramatic cliff. Stick around for the traditional Kekak fire dance at sunset. Just $4 for a ticket. Luxury resorts like Alila Villas cost upwards of $500 per night, but cozy guest houses start at just $25. Next, head north to Ubud, the spiritual center of the island. The drive takes about 2 hours. You'll pass through rice terraces, jungle roads, and sleepy villages. In Ubud, explore the Tagalang rice terraces, a breathtaking landscape that feels like a painting. Entry is around $1. Take a yoga class at the yoga barn. Try a $3 smoothie bowl and relax in a jungle view calf. Don't miss the sacred monkey forest where over 1,000 longtailed macocks roam freely. For something less touristy, explore the east coast. Stop by Terta Ganga, a royal water palace with koi filled pools and stone carvings. Entry is just $2. Visit Virgin Beach, a hidden white sand paradise with crystalclear waters. You can get fresh grilled fish at a beach shack for just $5. From Sonorport, take a 45minut speedboat to Nusupanita, a rugged, untouched island. Here you'll find Kelling King Beach, where the cliffs form the shape of a T-Rex. The hike down is steep, but the view unreal. Swim with manta rays at Manta Point or snorkel in vibrant coral gardens for about $30. Renting a scooter on Nusa Panita costs $7 a day, but roads are rough, so drive carefully. End your journey in Kang, Bali's trendy beach town. It's full of hip cafes, surf schools, and boutique shops. Take a surf lesson for $20. Sip coffee at beachfront cafes and enjoy the Bali sunset at Echo Beach. Luxury villas here go for $100 to $300 per night. But you can find great Airbnbs starting from $40. Transport scooter $5 per day. Taxes $10 to 15 per ride. Food. Street food starts at $2. Restaurants average $8 to $15. Stay budget $20 to $50. Mid-range $80 to $150. Luxury $300 plus. [Music] Budget travelers $40 to $80 per day. Mid-range $120 to $250. Luxury $400 plus. Flights from India start at $250 round trip. Europe or US $600 to $12. Best time to visit April to June and September to early November for fewer crowds, lush greenery, and lower prices. Avoid December to February if you don't like rain. Bali is not just a destination. It's a transformation. A place where you reconnect with nature, with people, with yourself. Whether you're walking barefoot on the beach, meditating in a jungle hut, or sipping coconut under a temple tree, this island changes you. Welcome to Bali. [Music]

Don't miss the sacred monkey forest where over 1,000 longtailed macocks roam freely
Renting a scooter on Nusa Panita costs $7 a day, but roads are rough, so drive carefully
Avoid December to February if you don't like rain
